title: SCHOOLCHILDREN’S MOVEMENT AND READINESS TO LEARN DURING EXTREME HEAT
keywords: aim 3 soplay-sn recess observation (n=91) and marty thermal assessments (n=5 over 5 weeks)

summary.hook: This concurrent mixed-methods study in 5 Phoenix Metro (AZ) elementary schools examined recess during extreme heat, focusing on (1) practices/costs of offering recess, and children’s (2) readiness to learn and (3) movement by heat exposure and recess setting.
summary.summary_30s: This concurrent mixed-methods study in 5 Phoenix Metro (AZ) elementary schools examined recess during extreme heat, focusing on (1) practices/costs of offering recess, and children’s (2) readiness to learn and (3) movement by heat exposure and recess setting. Data included 15 interviews, 981 questionnaires over 6 weeks, and 91 recess observations plus 5 thermal assessments over 5 weeks, among 5th graders (age 10–11; n=218) and staff (n=15). Results show air temperatures 91–109°F (33–43°C) and mean radiant temperatures 103°F (39°C) in tree shade to 163°F (73°C) on courts; readiness to learn was higher when recess was outside or in gyms vs classrooms (p<.05), and MVPA was 17% higher outdoors and 40% higher in gyms compared to classroom recess (p<.001), where children were mainly sedentary (71%).
talktrack_2m: Focus: Determine (1) common practices and costs of offering school recess during extreme heat, and differences in children’s (2) readiness to learn and (3) movement by heat exposure and setting.
Approach: Population: 5th grade students (age 10–11) and school staff. Primary outcome: Readiness to learn; movement/MVPA during recess; heat exposure metrics.

Why it matters: Authors’ stated conclusions: Schools face expensive infrastructure needs to support safe recess during extreme heat; keeping recess inside classrooms is associated with lower readiness to learn and high sedentary behavior; gyms and outdoor recess are associated with higher readiness and greater MVPA, and shade placement may not align with the most active outdoor spaces. Implications (poster-grounded): Investment priorities: schoolyard shade and dedicated indoor movement spaces (e.g., gyms) may support both learning readiness and physical activity during heat. Practice guidance need: lack of uniform extreme-heat recess guidance leads to variable practices; evidence supports reconsidering “classroom recess” defaults. Mechanism/explanation (ONLY if stated): Not stated on poster (the poster implies setting changes affect sitting vs MVPA and readiness). Connections to prior work (ONLY if referenced): Not stated on poster
key_numbers: Schools N=5; students n=218; staff n=15.
Questionnaires over 6 weeks; recess observations and thermal assessments over 5 weeks.
Air temperatures ranged 91–109°F (33–43°C); mean radiant temperatures ranged 103°F (39°C) under tree shade to 163°F (73°C) on courts.
Readiness to learn was higher when recess was outside or in gyms compared to classrooms (p<.05).
Most recess was outside (60%); 20% in classrooms; 20% in gyms.
takeaways: Heat exposure (mean radiant temperature) ranged from 103°F (39°C) under tree shade to 163°F (73°C) on courts.
Aim 1: Essential resources to maintain safe outdoor recess (e.g., shade structures, water bottle filling stations) required years of fundraising.
limitations: The poster does not list formal study limitations.
The poster leaves open this clarification: What specific NEWS/PANAS-C items or subscales were used to define “readiness to learn,” and what is the scoring range?

what_to_ask.question: Readiness was higher outside/gyms vs classrooms (p<.05): what is the effect size and how meaningful is it educationally?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This gets the presenter to interpret the main result instead of repeating the title.

what_to_ask.question: How did you account for clustering by school/class in analyses (mixed models structure)?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This probes confidence, assumptions, or limitations without sounding hostile.

what_to_ask.question: What practical guidance would you recommend to schools deciding between outdoor, gym, or classroom recess on hot days?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This opens a useful conversation about why the work matters or where it goes next.

collaboration_ideas.idea: Planning an intervention study (e.g., adding shade or structured gym recess) to test causal effects on MVPA and readiness
